fixes.whatChanged: Added 'id' to the SELECT clause (now 'id, user_id, expires_at') and changed the DELETE to .eq('id', resetData.id) so the consumed token is actually removed.
fixes.notes: Minimal patch. No callers depend on the previous (broken) behavior.

fixes.whatChanged: Added a private escapeHtml() helper and applied it to every translated-string interpolation inside the three htmlBody templates (verification, password reset, weekly reminder). Also exported isSupportedEmailLang() for downstream enum validation.
sideEffects: Email subject line and textBody intentionally left unescaped — they are not in an HTML context.
fixes.notes: Minimal patch. Plain-text parts and URL interpolations (link, logUrl, settingsUrl) are server-generated and not translated, so they are not escaped.

fixes.whatChanged: Replaced the hardcoded fallback 'dev-secret-change-in-production' with a fail-fast throw on missing or short (<32 char) JWT_SECRET. The server now refuses to boot without a real secret.
sideEffects: Any deployment that did not set JWT_SECRET will now crash on startup instead of silently running with a forgeable secret — this is the intended security outcome.
fixes.notes: Minimal patch. The existing startup warning at server/src/index.ts:179-184 is now a no-op (the throw fires before app.listen) but was left in place as a defense-in-depth signal in case auth.ts is ever refactored.

fixes.whatChanged: Imported isSupportedEmailLang from server/src/lib/email.js and added an enum-validation check on preferred_language right after destructuring. Returns 400 with detail 'Unsupported preferred_language value' on invalid input. null (auto-detect) is still allowed.
fixes.notes: Minimal patch. The validation runs before any DB write, so invalid input never reaches the upsert.

fixes.whatChanged: Added a top-of-file SECURITY comment explaining that req.supabase is a service-role client (RLS bypass) and that all route handlers MUST scope queries by req.userId. No code behavior changed.
fixes.notes: Minimal patch — documentation only, as the bug is a forward-looking defense-in-depth gap rather than an exploitable code path.

fixes.whatChanged: 1) Added session_ttl_days INT NOT NULL DEFAULT 30 column to refresh_tokens (CREATE TABLE + idempotent ALTER TABLE). 2) createRefreshToken now stores session_ttl_days in the insert row. 3) The /refresh handler reads the old token's session_ttl_days and passes it to createRefreshToken, so a rememberMe=false session no longer escalates to 30 days on the first refresh.
sideEffects: Existing refresh_tokens rows without session_ttl_days will get the default 30 from the idempotent ALTER; the /refresh handler falls back to 30 via the nullish coalesce for legacy rows that pre-date the column.
fixes.notes: Migration is idempotent and safe to run on a populated table. The login handler already passed the correct TTL; only the persistence and the refresh read were missing.
